body_txt: One of the most severe forms of Typecasting , in which an actor is not only defined as a certain character archetype, but in their most famous role, much to their frustration as they try to move on with their career. This is most certainly a double-edged sword, as it often guarantees that the performer will be famous for some time to come, but on the other hand, that role may end up stifling the performer's future acting prospects, as they may get rejected for other roles that may be seen as being against type . This can be tough on actors who actually want to expand their repertoire with diverse roles. As viewers and historians, it can sometimes be difficult to tell the difference between an actor who lucked out by getting one iconic role, and an actor whose career was hampered by an over-association with it. Typecasting may have been more common in the early days of television as a holdover from motion pictures. It was common in the 1930s and '40s for a movie actor who was not a lead to make a career out of playing the same type of character. This seems to disproportionately happen to actors from sci-fi and fantasy works, possibly because some casting agencies may feel their credibility as a "serious" actor has been hurt by working on such shows . For extra bad luck, the character may have a Catchphrase the actor will be asked to repeat incessantly, or even worse be a Phrase Catcher and have to endure hearing it yelled at them by every Loony Fan . Named for the 1975 autobiography by none other than Leonard Nimoy . An autobiography that, incidentally, didn't actually say what everyone thought it said because of the title. Before adding examples, remember that this trope is not (or at least should not be) a list of every actor with an iconic role. They must specifically be unhappy being associated with that role because they see it as hindering their broader career. See also …But I Play One on TV and One Hit Wonder: Acting . Compare Adam Westing and Never Live It Down . Contrast I Am Not Leonard Nimoy . Overlaps with Contractual Purity when an actor is best known for a role in family-friendly fare and will alienate their fanbase if their next project is insufficiently 'wholesome'. Finally, see Role Association for the Just for Fun version. Not to be confused with I Am Not Shazam . For the writer equivalent, see Author Usurpation .
